refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligationâ€”enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelopeâ€”without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 â‰¤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to duty cycle and site conditions, not a functional requirement of the system itself. This separation allows design parameters (e.g., hydraulic response time, travel speed envelope, load handling efficiency) to be evaluated independently of operational context.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and representative site conditions, the NGHE solution's subsystems (propulsion, load handling, operator interface, autonomy/tele-operation) do not introduce delays that prevent cycle completion within CT_target. Measure end-to-end cycle time across multiple runs; confirm p95 â‰¤ CT_target. Identify and quantify any system-imposed bottlenecks (e.g., hydraulic lag, travel speed limits, load positioning time) that would prevent target achievement.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable load acquisition within a defined time envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable travel from load site to dump site within a defined time envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable load release/dump within a defined time envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize idle/transition time between load, travel, and dump phases.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose the loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle into sequential sub-operations (load, travel, dump) with independent timing budgets that sum to CT_target, each optimized for its operational constraints.
DP2: Implement a centralized cycle controller that orchestrates all sub-operations via state machine logic, with feedback loops to detect and compensate for delays in real-time.

DP3: Design the powertrain and actuation system to deliver peak performance (acceleration, speed, force) sufficient to complete each sub-operation within its allocated time budget under worst-case site conditions.
DP4: Establish a predictive load-balancing strategy that pre-stages equipment state (e.g., bucket position, engine RPM, transmission gear) to minimize transition delays between sub-operations.

DP5: Implement adaptive cycle timing that monitors actual cycle performance (p95 metric) and adjusts operational parameters (speed profiles, dwell times) to maintain CT_target compliance across varying duty cycles and site conditions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP2 + DP3 + DP5 (integrated approach): Decompose the cycle into independent timing budgets (DP1), orchestrate via state machine control (DP2), ensure powertrain capability (DP3), and adapt execution in real-time to maintain p95 â‰¤ CT_target (DP5). DP4 is deferred as a secondary optimization.

potential_sources: DP1 â†” DP3: Timing budgets must be validated against actual powertrain performance; loose coupling if budgets are conservative and powertrain is over-specified.

DP2 â†” DP5: State machine logic and adaptive timing both influence cycle execution; risk of conflicting control decisions if feedback loops are not clearly prioritized (e.g., which takes precedence: state transitions or adaptive speed adjustments?).
DP3 â†” DP5: Powertrain capability ceiling and adaptive parameter adjustment must be coordinated; adaptive logic cannot request performance beyond design limits without degrading reliability.

Implicit coupling via site conditions: All DPs depend on accurate characterization of 'worst-case site conditions' (terrain, load variability, ambient); if conditions exceed assumptions, timing budgets and adaptive logic both fail.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP set is logically sound but exhibits moderate coupling through feedback and constraint interdependencies. Coupling is manageable if: (1) timing budgets are derived from validated powertrain performance data, (2) state machine and adaptive logic have clear ownership and priority rules, (3) site condition assumptions are explicitly documented and validated during commissioning.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Cycle time variability is primarily driven by operational delays (load density, travel distance, dump conditions) rather than equipment failures or unplanned stops. RISK: If equipment reliability is poor, p95 metric will be dominated by failure recovery time, not cycle optimization.

ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (terrain, load mass, ambient temperature) can be characterized and bounded during design. RISK: Extreme or unanticipated conditions (e.g., muddy terrain, overloaded bucket) will violate timing budgets; adaptive logic may not recover.
ASSUMPTION: Feedback sensors (cycle time, sub-operation duration, powertrain load) are available and reliable. RISK: Sensor failures or delays will corrupt adaptive logic and state machine decisions.

ASSUMPTION: The customer-defined CT_target is achievable with the selected powertrain and actuation technology. RISK: If CT_target is aggressive relative to physical constraints, no DP can satisfy it; requirement may need renegotiation.

ASSUMPTION: p95 metric is the appropriate acceptance criterion (i.e., 5% of cycles can exceed CT_target). RISK: If customer expects p100 (all cycles â‰¤ CT_target), the design must be more conservative, increasing cost and complexity.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not impose constraints that conflict with cycle time optimization (e.g., mandatory dwell times, speed limits). RISK: If safety policies force longer cycle times, CT_target may be unachievable.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine the FR to explicitly state: (1) the acceptable p-percentile (p95, p99, p100) and tolerance band around CT_target, (2) the range of site conditions (terrain type, load mass, ambient temperature) under which CT_target must be met, (3) the role of safety and operational policies in constraining cycle execution. Decompose DP2 and DP5 further to clarify the control architecture: specify whether adaptive logic operates within the state machine (as a parameter tuning layer) or as a separate feedback loop, and define priority rules for conflict resolution. Validate DP3 (powertrain capability) against the timing budgets derived from DP1 using simulation or prototype testing before committing to detailed design. Document the coupling between DPs explicitly in the design specification to enable traceability and risk management.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated cycle decomposition, timing budget management, state machine orchestration, powertrain capability validation, and real-time execution adaptation for cycle time control

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives cycle requirements and execution commands from Vehicle or Teleoperation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports cycle state, timing metrics (p95 CT), budget utilization, and adaptation decisions

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives powertrain capability constraints; exports safety-critical execution decisions and constraint violations

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates with remote systems for teleoperation coordination and distributed state synchronization if required

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. DP1 (timing budgets), DP2 (state machine), DP3 (powertrain validation), and DP5 (real-time adaptation) are all core to NGHE's mission of autonomous cycle execution. Timing orchestration, state management, and adaptive control are naturally cohesive within a single solution module. Minor diffusion: powertrain capability data flows in via SafetyAlert; telemetry flows out; tasking commands flow in.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical platform abstraction; it should not own cycle timing logic, state machines, or real-time adaptation algorithms. These DPs are algorithmic and control-oriented, not vehicle-specific. High diffusion: DP1, DP2, DP5 would leak into Vehicle's responsibility boundary, violating separation of concerns.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a remote command/monitoring interface, not an execution engine. Cycle timing budgets, state machine orchestration, and real-time adaptation belong in autonomous execution logic, not in a teleoperation module. High diffusion: DP2 and DP5 would be misplaced in a remote interface layer.

Weak fit. Safety Services should validate and enforce constraints (DP3 powertrain capability checks), but should not own the full cycle orchestration, timing budgets, or real-time adaptation logic. DP1, DP2, DP5 are execution concerns, not safety validation concerns. Moderate diffusion: DP3 fits well; DP1, DP2, DP5 would diffuse into execution logic outside Safety Services.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The integrated DP (DP1 + DP2 + DP3 + DP5) is fundamentally an autonomous execution control strategy. NGHE Solution is the natural owner because it encapsulates the cycle execution engine, timing orchestration, state management, and adaptive control logic. Powertrain capability validation (DP3) is a constraint input that NGHE Solution consumes via SafetyAlert; it does not require ownership by Safety Services. Real-time adaptation (DP5) is an execution-time optimization that belongs in the execution engine, not in a separate service. This boundary minimizes diffusion and maximizes cohesion.
containment_risks: Risk: If powertrain capability constraints (DP3) are complex or frequently updated, Safety Services may need to own constraint definition and validation logic, creating a split responsibility. Mitigation: Define a clear SafetyAlert interface contract that NGHE Solution consumes; Safety Services owns constraint logic, NGHE Solution owns constraint application.

Risk: Real-time adaptation (DP5) may require low-latency feedback from Vehicle telemetry. If Vehicle module does not expose fine-grained telemetry, NGHE Solution may struggle to adapt quickly. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry interface provides cycle-level metrics (e.g., segment completion times, resource utilization) at sufficient frequency.

Risk: State machine complexity (DP2) may grow if cycle variants or failure modes are not well-scoped. Mitigation: Establish clear state definitions and transitions in design phase; defer DP4 (secondary optimization) to avoid scope creep.

Risk: If Teleoperation requires real-time intervention in cycle execution, NGHE Solution must support preemption or override via Tasking interface. Mitigation: Define override semantics and safety constraints in Tasking interface contract.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is recommended. The integrated DP (DP1 + DP2 + DP3 + DP5) is well-suited to NGHE Solution. However, clarify the boundary between NGHE Solution and Safety Services: NGHE Solution owns execution orchestration and real-time adaptation; Safety Services owns constraint definition, validation, and safety-critical decision approval. This separation ensures that DP3 (powertrain capability) is enforced as a constraint, not as an execution algorithm, reducing diffusion and improving modularity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The integrated DP (DP1 + DP2 + DP3 + DP5) directly and completely addresses the FR. DP1 (timing budgets) and DP2 (state machine orchestration) provide the structural decomposition needed to execute the loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle. DP3 (powertrain capability validation) ensures feasibility within physical constraints. DP5 (real-time adaptation) directly targets the acceptance criterion p95 â‰¤ CT_target by enabling dynamic execution adjustment. The deferral of DP4 (secondary optimization) is justified as it is not required to meet the primary cycle time target. The DP set is necessary and sufficient for FR satisfaction.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion via SafetyAlert interface: DP3 (powertrain capability validation) logic is consumed by NGHE Solution but defined/owned by Safety Services. Mitigation: Establish explicit interface contract defining constraint format, update frequency, and validation responsibility boundaries.

Potential diffusion if Telemetry interface does not provide sufficient granularity: DP5 (real-time adaptation) requires cycle-level metrics (segment completion times, resource utilization) at high frequency. If Vehicle module exports only coarse-grained telemetry, NGHE Solution may lack data for effective adaptation. Mitigation: Define Telemetry interface SLA specifying metric types, sampling rates, and latency bounds.

State machine complexity risk (DP2): If cycle variants, failure modes, or override semantics are not well-scoped, state machine ownership may diffuse across NGHE Solution and Teleoperation. Mitigation: Establish clear state definitions and transition rules in design phase; document override/preemption semantics in Tasking interface contract.

Execution-time constraint enforcement (DP3): If Safety Services must approve real-time execution decisions (e.g., powertrain limit violations), synchronous validation may create latency bottlenecks. Mitigation: Pre-compute and cache constraint envelopes; use asynchronous validation for non-critical decisions; define safety-critical decision approval path in SafetyAlert interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. The module boundary is well-defined: NGHE Solution owns cycle execution orchestration (DP1, DP2), real-time adaptation (DP5), and constraint application (DP3 consumption). External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clearly delineated. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules show poor containment (0.35, 0.28, 0.45 respectively) for the integrated DP, confirming that NGHE Solution is the correct owner. The recommended separation of concerns is sound: NGHE Solution owns execution logic; Safety Services owns constraint definition and validation; Vehicle owns platform abstraction; Teleoperation owns remote command/monitoring. This architecture minimizes algorithmic diffusion and maintains clear ownership boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'p95 â‰¤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly measurable via the Telemetry interface (cycle state, timing metrics, p95 CT, budget utilization). NGHE Solution exports these metrics, enabling objective verification. Real-time adaptation (DP5) is observable through telemetry logs and adaptation decision records. Powertrain capability validation (DP3) can be verified by cross-checking SafetyAlert constraint inputs against execution decisions. State machine transitions (DP2) are traceable via state logs. Timing budget allocation (DP1) is verifiable through segment completion time analysis. No hidden or unmeasurable requirements exist.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to NGHE Solution with explicit responsibility for cycle execution within CT_target. The allocation is supported by four design parameters with distinct concerns: DP1 (timing structure), DP2 (control logic), DP3 (constraint enforcement), DP5 (adaptive optimization). Each DP maps to a specific aspect of the FR. External dependencies are explicit: Tasking (input), Telemetry (output), SafetyAlert (constraint input/decision output), Network Link (coordination). The worksite safety plan and operational policies are consumed via SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces, ensuring compliance is traceable. No ambiguity in allocation or responsibility boundaries.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the SafetyAlert interface contract: Define constraint data structure (powertrain limits, safety thresholds), update frequency, validation responsibility (Safety Services owns definition; NGHE Solution owns application), and approval semantics for safety-critical decisions. This clarifies the DP3 boundary and reduces diffusion risk.

Establish Telemetry interface SLA: Specify cycle-level metrics required for DP5 (real-time adaptation), sampling rates, latency bounds, and data format. Ensure Vehicle module commits to providing sufficient granularity for adaptive control.

Document state machine design (DP2) in design phase: Define all cycle states, transitions, failure modes, and override/preemption semantics. Establish clear ownership: NGHE Solution owns state machine logic; Teleoperation owns override commands (via Tasking interface); Safety Services owns constraint-driven state transitions (via SafetyAlert).

Define Tasking interface override semantics: Clarify how Teleoperation can preempt or override cycle execution, what safety constraints apply, and how NGHE Solution handles conflicting commands. This prevents diffusion of execution logic into Teleoperation.

Establish DP5 (real-time adaptation) algorithm specification: Define adaptation triggers (e.g., segment time deviation thresholds), adjustment mechanisms (e.g., speed modulation, segment reordering), and limits (e.g., maximum deviation from nominal cycle). This ensures adaptation logic is contained within NGHE Solution and not scattered across modules.

Defer DP4 (secondary optimization) formally: Document that DP4 is out of scope for initial NGHE Solution release. Establish a change control process for future optimization work to prevent scope creep and state machine complexity explosion.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, indicating the current decomposition is logically sound and architecturally feasible. However, the gate results expose six critical diffusion risks and interface contract gaps that must be resolved before proceeding to detailed design or sub-FR derivation. Given the high safety criticality of this system, revising the current level to formalize interface contracts, clarify ownership boundaries, and document state machine semantics is essential. Proceeding to sub-FR derivation without resolving these gaps risks architectural diffusion, safety validation failures, and rework during implementation. The recommended revisions are architectural (not functional) and must be completed at this level to establish a stable foundation for lower-level decomposition.

revise.why: The DP set is functionally correct and necessary/sufficient for FR satisfaction. However, the interface contracts and ownership boundaries between DP1 (timing budgets), DP2 (state machine orchestration), DP3 (powertrain capability validation), and DP5 (real-time adaptation) are under-specified. Specifically: (1) DP3 diffuses across NGHE Solution and Safety Services via SafetyAlert interfaceâ€”requires explicit contract defining constraint format, validation responsibility, and approval semantics. (2) DP5 depends on Telemetry interface granularityâ€”requires SLA specifying cycle-level metrics, sampling rates, and latency bounds. (3) DP2 state machine ownership may diffuse if cycle variants and override semantics are not well-scopedâ€”requires formal state definition and transition rules. (4) DP3 execution-time constraint enforcement may create latency bottlenecks if Safety Services must synchronously approve decisionsâ€”requires pre-computation strategy and asynchronous validation path. Revising the DP specifications to include explicit interface contracts, data structure definitions, and responsibility boundaries will eliminate diffusion risks and enable clean sub-FR derivation in the next iteration.

assumptions: Safety Services will provide powertrain constraint envelopes (DP3 input) via SafetyAlert interface with sufficient frequency and granularity to enable real-time execution decisions.
Vehicle module will export cycle-level telemetry (DP5 input) with sampling rates and latency compatible with real-time adaptation control loops.
Teleoperation override commands (DP2 input) will be infrequent and non-conflicting with autonomous cycle execution logic.
State machine complexity will remain bounded to loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle variants; no major failure mode recovery or multi-cycle orchestration is in scope for initial release.
Real-time adaptation (DP5) will operate within pre-defined adjustment limits (speed modulation, segment reordering) and will not require Safety Services approval for each decision.
Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable and will be communicated via SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces without mid-cycle changes.
open_risks: SafetyAlert interface contract not formalized: DP3 (powertrain capability validation) logic ownership may diffuse if constraint data structure, update frequency, and validation responsibility are not explicitly defined. Risk: Safety Services and NGHE Solution may have conflicting interpretations of constraint enforcement, leading to validation failures or unsafe execution decisions.

Telemetry interface SLA not established: DP5 (real-time adaptation) effectiveness depends on cycle-level metric granularity and latency. Risk: If Vehicle module provides only coarse-grained telemetry, NGHE Solution cannot detect segment-level deviations, rendering adaptation ineffective and failing the p95 â‰¤ CT_target acceptance criterion.

State machine design not documented: DP2 (state machine orchestration) ownership may diffuse across NGHE Solution and Teleoperation if cycle states, transitions, failure modes, and override semantics are not formally defined. Risk: Ambiguous state definitions may lead to conflicting execution decisions or unsafe preemption behavior.

DP3 execution-time constraint enforcement bottleneck: If Safety Services must synchronously validate real-time execution decisions (e.g., powertrain limit violations), latency may violate cycle timing budgets. Risk: Synchronous validation may cause missed segment deadlines, increasing p95 CT and failing acceptance criterion.

DP5 adaptation algorithm not specified: Real-time adaptation triggers, adjustment mechanisms, and limits are not formally defined. Risk: Adaptation logic may diffuse across NGHE Solution and other modules, or may be implemented inconsistently, reducing effectiveness and complicating verification.

Tasking interface override semantics unclear: How Teleoperation preempts cycle execution, what safety constraints apply, and how NGHE Solution handles conflicting commands are not documented. Risk: Unsafe override behavior or execution logic diffusion into Teleoperation module.
evidence_needed: SafetyAlert interface contract specification: Define powertrain constraint data structure (limits, thresholds, validity windows), update frequency (e.g., per-cycle, per-segment), validation responsibility assignment (Safety Services owns definition; NGHE Solution owns application), and approval semantics for safety-critical decisions (synchronous vs. asynchronous, pre-computed vs. real-time).

Telemetry interface SLA: Specify cycle-level metrics required for DP5 (segment completion times, resource utilization, deviation flags), sampling rates (e.g., per-segment or per-100ms), latency bounds (e.g., <500ms end-to-end), and data format (JSON, protobuf, etc.). Confirm Vehicle module commitment to provide this granularity.

State machine design document (DP2): Enumerate all cycle states (load, travel, dump, idle, error, override), transitions (normal, failure, preemption), failure modes (sensor loss, powertrain limit, safety alert), and override/preemption semantics (Teleoperation command priority, safety constraint override rules). Assign ownership: NGHE Solution owns state machine logic; Teleoperation owns override commands; Safety Services owns constraint-driven transitions.

DP3 constraint enforcement architecture: Define pre-computation strategy for constraint envelopes (e.g., cache powertrain limits at cycle start), asynchronous validation path for non-critical decisions, and synchronous approval path for safety-critical decisions. Specify latency bounds for each path and confirm compatibility with cycle timing budgets.

DP5 real-time adaptation algorithm specification: Define adaptation triggers (e.g., segment time deviation >10% of budget), adjustment mechanisms (speed modulation range, segment reordering rules), limits (maximum deviation from nominal cycle), and decision frequency (per-segment, per-100ms, etc.). Confirm algorithm is contained within NGHE Solution and not scattered across modules.

Tasking interface override semantics: Define override command types (pause, resume, abort, manual control), safety constraints that apply during override (e.g., powertrain limits still enforced), conflict resolution rules (e.g., safety constraint overrides Teleoperation command), and NGHE Solution response behavior (state transition, logging, notification).

DP4 deferral decision record: Formally document that secondary optimization (DP4) is out of scope for initial NGHE Solution release. Establish change control process for future optimization work to prevent scope creep and state machine complexity explosion.

Safety criticality assessment: Confirm that all safety-critical decisions (powertrain limit enforcement, override handling, failure mode recovery) are owned by Safety Services or NGHE Solution with explicit safety validation responsibility. Identify any gaps in safety ownership or validation.
